headline: Big companies got AI first. That's backwards.
fb: Big companies got AI first. That's backwards.
It isn't the Fortune 500 that loses a $4,000 job because nobody picked up at 5:15 on a Friday. It's the plumber. The salon. The family restaurant that had two people on and forty phone calls.
Main Street feels every dropped call in its bank account the same week it happens. And Main Street is the one place that got handed AI last, in the form of a chatbot bubble that can't do anything.

So I started posting here. Every week I'm going to give away the specific moves that put money back in a small business. Free, no course, no funnel. Some of them involve AI. Half of them are just a switch you already pay for and never turned on.
Tell me what you do in the comments and I'll tell you where I'd start.

headline: Comment your industry. I'll give you the one AI move that pays for itself.
fb: Comment your industry below and I'll give you the one AI move I'd make first in your specific business. Free, no pitch, I'm not going to DM you.
Not "use AI to save time." An actual move. The thing I'd do on Monday morning if it were my company.
Roofing is different from a med spa. A two-truck landscaping outfit is different from a twelve-chair salon. Generic advice is why most owners have concluded this stuff isn't for them.
So tell me: what do you do, how many people, and what's the part of the week that makes you want to quit?
I answer every one of these. Might take me a day, but I get to all of them.
firstComment: Everything I post here goes deeper in the free group: {{GROUP_URL}}. No pitching, no spam, just the moves that put money back in a small business.

headline: Five fields on your Google profile that almost nobody fills in.
fb: Your Google Business Profile is the most valuable piece of real estate you own and most owners set it up once in 2021 and never touched it again.
Five things to fix this afternoon. All free, all take about twenty minutes together.
1. Services. Not just "plumber." List every specific job you do, in the words customers use. Water heater replacement. Slab leak. Garbage disposal. Each one is a search someone is typing right now.
2. Photos. Yours are three years old and there are four of them. Add ten from recent jobs. Profiles with fresh photos get meaningfully more calls, and it's the cheapest thing on this list.
3. Hours, including holidays. Wrong hours generate one-star reviews from people who drove out to a closed door.
4. The Q&A section. You can ask and answer your own questions. Nobody knows this. Put your eight most common questions in there yourself.
5. Products or services with prices, even ranges. "Starting at" is fine. People filter you out for not saying anything far more often than for saying a number.
Which of the five is worst on yours? Be honest, mine was the photos for years.

headline: "We'll get back to you" is where most small business revenue goes to die.
fb: "We'll get back to you." That sentence is where most small business revenue quietly goes to die.
Not because anyone lied. Because the person who said it went to the next job, and the note was on a scrap of paper in a truck, and it's Thursday now.
Here's the three-text sequence that fixes it. Steal it, it costs nothing.
Text 1, same day: "Hey [name], Sarah from [business]. Great talking with you. Sending the quote over tonight." (Send it that night. This is the whole trick.)
Text 2, day 3: "Just making sure that quote landed, any questions on it?"
Text 3, day 8: "No pressure at all, just closing the loop. Was it price or timing? Genuinely helps me either way."
That last one is the one people skip and it's the most valuable. Half of them answer it honestly, and now you know something real about your pricing that no consultant could tell you.
How many days go by before you follow up on a quote right now? Nobody's judging, I want to see the real spread.

headline: Four things AI genuinely cannot do for your business.
fb: I build AI for small businesses, so here are four things it genuinely cannot do. Anyone telling you otherwise is selling.
1. It cannot make a judgment call about a customer. Whether to eat the cost on a job that went sideways, whether this person is worth keeping. That's you. That will stay you.
2. It cannot fix a business that doesn't answer the phone. If the problem is a habit, software just helps you miss things faster and more professionally.
3. It cannot know your prices, your area, or your standards unless somebody sits down and tells it. The setup is real work. Anyone promising "plug it in, done" has never done it.
4. It cannot replace being the person people trust. In a small business the owner IS the brand. Automating the parts nobody sees is smart. Automating the relationship is how you lose it.
What it's actually good at is the boring middle. The calls at 8pm. The follow-up nobody sent. The paperwork on Sunday. The eight questions you answer forty times a week.
That's not a sexy pitch, but it's the honest one.
What's the thing you'd never hand to a machine, no matter how good it got?

headline: Teardown Tuesday. Drop your business. I'll find the leak on video. Free.
fb: TEARDOWN TUESDAY. Here's how it works.
Drop your business name and website in the comments. I pick one. Then I pull up your site, your Google profile, and I call your business on camera, live, as a customer.
Then I tell you exactly where you're losing money and the one thing I'd fix first. On video, free, and you keep the video.
Two promises. I will never mock anybody, we're on the same side against the problem. And I always give you the free version of the fix, even when there's a paid version.
Only volunteers. I don't do this to businesses that didn't raise their hand.
Who wants it? First good one I see, I shoot it tonight.
firstComment: Everything I post here goes deeper in the free group: {{GROUP_URL}}. No pitching, no spam, just the moves that put money back in a small business.

headline: Your voicemail greeting was recorded by an employee who quit two years ago.
fb: Go listen to your own voicemail greeting right now. I'll wait.

Here's what I hear when I call small businesses all day. A greeting recorded by somebody who doesn't work there anymore. Hours that are wrong. A mailbox that's full. A robot voice reading back a phone number because nobody ever recorded anything at all.
Meanwhile the person calling is standing in a flooded kitchen at 6pm deciding who to give four thousand dollars to.
Fix it in ten minutes, free:
Record it yourself, in your voice. Say the business name, say you're sorry you missed them, and then say this: "Text this same number and we'll get right back to you."
Then actually watch your texts.
People who would never leave a voicemail will happily send a text. You just recovered a chunk of the work you were losing, before lunch, for nothing.
Go listen to yours and tell me how bad it is. I want to hear the worst one.

headline: Paste your last 50 customer texts into AI and ask it this one question.
fb: Prompt of the week. Copy this exactly.
Scroll back through your last fifty customer texts. Copy them, paste them into ChatGPT or Claude, and say:

"These are real messages from my customers. Find the questions I answer most often, ranked. For each one, write the answer in plain language a customer would understand, in my voice based on how I already replied."
What you get back is your FAQ page, written for you, in words your actual customers actually use. Not marketing words. Their words.
Put those answers on your website. Put them in your voicemail greeting. Put them anywhere someone can reach them at 9pm without you.
Right now you are being paid nothing to personally be your own FAQ page, forty times a week, usually while driving.
Run it and tell me your number one question. I'm collecting these by industry and the pattern is fascinating.

headline: "You're too expensive" almost never means you're too expensive.
fb: "You're too expensive" almost never means you're too expensive.
It usually means one of three other things, and they all have a different fix.
"I don't understand what I'm getting." Your quote was a number on a line. The guy who won showed them what's included and why the cheap version fails in two years.
"I don't trust that you'll show up." Price is the only thing they can compare, so they compare it. Reviews, photos, and answering the phone fix this one, not discounting.
"I wasn't ready to buy today." Nothing was wrong with your price. They needed two more weeks and you never followed up, so they bought from whoever was still around when they were ready.
Almost nobody's real problem is the number. Dropping your price to solve a trust problem just makes you a cheaper business with the same trust problem.
What's the objection you hear most? I'll tell you which of the three it actually is.

headline: The contractor who calls back first wins the job most of the time.
fb: The one who calls back first wins the job most of the time. Not the best one. The first one.

Here's what people get wrong about this. They think the customer is comparing three quotes carefully over a week. They're not. They filled out three forms in four minutes, they're sitting there with the phone in their hand, and they are actively deciding right now.
That window is minutes wide.
Most small businesses call back the next morning. By then somebody else already came out, quoted it, and the customer is relieved it's handled.
You do not have to be better than your competition. You have to be awake at the same time as your customer.
Honest question, no judgment: how long does it actually take you to call a new lead back? Not how long you'd like it to take. The real number.

headline: Win Friday. What worked this week? Big or stupidly small.
fb: WIN FRIDAY. What worked this week?
Doesn't have to be big. Especially shouldn't be big. Post the small one.
You finally fixed your review link. You texted an old quote and it turned into a job. You said no to a customer who was going to be a nightmare. You got out of the shop before six once.

I'll go first: I called eleven businesses this week for a teardown and two of them answered. Two out of eleven. Every time I run this I'm reminded the bar on Main Street is on the floor, and that's genuinely good news for anyone willing to just pick up the phone.
Your turn. What went right? 👇
firstComment: Everything I post here goes deeper in the free group: {{GROUP_URL}}. No pitching, no spam, just the moves that put money back in a small business.

headline: Export last month's calendar into AI and ask where your week actually went.
fb: Export last month's calendar to a spreadsheet, drop it into AI, and ask this:
"Categorize every entry as revenue-generating, admin, or travel. Show me the hours and percentage in each. Then tell me what a normal week actually looks like for me."
Almost every owner who runs this finds the same two things. Way more travel than they thought, and one recurring meeting or errand that eats half a day a week and generates nothing.

You cannot fix a week you've never actually looked at. You've been running on a feeling about where your time goes, and the feeling is wrong in a specific direction: you think you spend more time making money than you do.
Takes fifteen minutes. Free. It's the most uncomfortable and most useful thing on this list.
If you run it, come back and post your percentage. I want to see the spread across industries.

headline: A restaurant's phone rings 40 times on a Friday. Nobody can pick up.
fb: A busy restaurant's phone rings forty times on a Friday night and almost nobody picks up. Not from laziness. Because the phone and the floor need the same two hands, and the people in front of you are the ones who are already here.
Every one of those rings was a reservation, a to-go order, or a catering lead. Catering is the one that hurts. That's a four-figure call and it went to whoever answered.
You cannot hire your way out of a Friday rush. Nobody's staffing an extra body just to sit by a phone.

The free version: put your reservation link and your online ordering link everywhere the phone is listed, and change your greeting so it tells people that in the first six seconds instead of after a menu tree.
Restaurant folks, what's the call you hate missing most? I'm guessing catering.

headline: Stop asking AI to write your marketing. Point it at your invoices.
fb: Stop asking AI to write your marketing. It's the first thing everybody tries and it's the worst thing it does for you.

Everyone's feed is full of the same beige AI paragraphs now and customers can smell it from across the room. You are not going to out-post the internet, and honestly nobody hired a plumber because of a caption.
Point it at the stuff nobody sees.

Your invoices, to find the job type that quietly loses money. Your calendar, to find where the week actually goes. Your missed calls, to find out what a Tuesday costs you. Your bad reviews, all at once, to find the one pattern. Your old quotes, to find the money already sitting there.
That's where it earns. It's boring, which is exactly why it's still available.
The best AI use case in your business isn't a clever post. It's the fact that nobody called Mrs. Henderson back.
Disagree with me. I want to hear it.

headline: Google says replying to reviews affects your ranking. Most owners reply to none.
fb: Google says plainly that responding to reviews affects your local ranking. Most small businesses reply to none of them, or only the angry ones, which is the worst possible pattern to show a stranger reading your profile.
Fifteen minutes a week, free:

Pull the week's new reviews. Drop them into AI and ask for a short, warm, specific reply to each one that mentions the actual thing they said. Then read every single one before you post it and fix anything that sounds like a machine.
That last part is not optional. A reply that reads like a robot is worse than no reply, and people can tell instantly. "Thank you for your valuable feedback" makes you look like a cable company.
For the bad ones: acknowledge, don't argue, and take it to a phone call. Anyone reading it later is judging how you handled it, not who was right.
How many reviews are sitting on your profile right now with no reply? Go look. It's usually more than you'd guess.

headline: "I'll just do it myself" is the most expensive sentence in small business.
fb: "I'll just do it myself, it's faster" is the most expensive sentence in small business, and every good operator says it.
It's true, by the way. It IS faster this one time. That's what makes it so hard to stop.

But here's the arithmetic. If your work is worth $95 an hour and you spend six hours a week on scheduling, invoicing, and answering the same questions, that's roughly $570 a week you paid yourself to do $18-an-hour work. Call it $27,000 a year.
You wouldn't hire someone at your rate to do that job. But you hired you.

The move isn't to hire a person. For most of you it's not the right time and you know it. The move is to ask one question about every task on that list: does this need my judgment, or does it just need my attention?
Judgment stays. Attention goes.
What's the one thing you keep doing yourself that you know you shouldn't? 👇

headline: Tell me the worst part of your week. I'll tell you if it's fixable.
fb: New version of the Monday question. Tell me the worst part of your week and I'll tell you honestly whether it's fixable, and how.
Not "what would you automate." Most owners don't know, and that question makes people feel like they should already have an answer.

Just tell me the thing you dread. The Sunday invoicing. The scheduling phone tag. The one customer. The quotes you never send. The part where you get home and remember four people you didn't call back.
I'll give you a straight answer, including "that one's not a software problem" when that's the truth, which is more often than people expect.
Free, no DM, no pitch. I just find these conversations more useful than anything I could write on my own.
Go. 👇
firstComment: Everything I post here goes deeper in the free group: {{GROUP_URL}}. No pitching, no spam, just the moves that put money back in a small business.

headline: Drowning in bad applicants is a job-post problem, not a labor problem.
fb: If you're drowning in unqualified applicants, that's not the labor market. That's your job post.
Take your current one, paste it into AI, and say: "Rewrite this so the wrong people screen themselves out. Be specific and honest about the hard parts, the schedule, and the pay range."
Most job posts are written to attract everybody. That's the whole mistake. A good job post repels.
It says the truck leaves at 6:15, you will be in an attic in August, we drug test, here is the actual number, and here is what you'll be making in two years if you're good.
You'll get fewer applications and better ones, and you'll stop burning a week interviewing people who were never going to take it.
Say the hard part out loud. The right person reads it and thinks "finally, somebody honest."
What's the thing about your job you'd warn someone about? That's your opening line.

headline: She tried to book at 9:14pm. Your desk left at 6. She booked elsewhere by 9:16.
fb: Your best client tried to book at 9:14 at night. Your front desk went home at six. She booked somewhere else at 9:16.

This is the part that owners in booking businesses get wrong about after-hours. It's not that she'll call back tomorrow. It's that the decision was happening right then, on the couch, phone in hand, in a two-minute window that opened because she finally had a second to herself.
The sale doesn't wait for business hours. It evaporates.

Salons, spas, dental, anything people book for themselves: pull your booking data and look at when requests actually come in. There's a spike after 8pm and it's bigger than you think. Then look at what your phone does during that spike.
The free move: make sure online booking is one tap from your Instagram bio, your Google profile, and your voicemail greeting. Not buried on a website. One tap.
The most expensive employee is the one who isn't there at 9pm.

eyebrow: MAIN STREET AI · UNDER THE HOOD
headline: This is what an AI answering your phone actually sounds like now.
fb: Whatever picture you have in your head of "AI answering the phone," it's probably four years out of date. It's not the press-1-for-billing tree.

It picks up on the first ring, it knows your hours and your service area, it answers the eight questions you get all week, it books straight into your calendar, and it texts you the details before you're off the ladder.

I'm not posting this to sell you one. I'm posting it because this is what your customer at 8pm is comparing you to now, whether you like it or not. Somebody in your market already has this and is quietly eating the after-hours work.
You can call one and hear it yourself. Link in the comments, free, no signup, it just rings your phone and talks to you.
Then tell me honestly what you thought. I want the skeptical takes too, they're usually the most useful ones.
firstComment: You can call one and hear it: modernmustardseed.com/sidekick. Free, no signup, it just calls you.

headline: There's money sitting in your old quotes and you've never gone back for it.
fb: Slow afternoon project that costs nothing and works almost every time.
Pull every quote from the last twelve months that never turned into a job. Send one text. Not a pitch:
"Hey, it's Sarah at [business]. We quoted you back in March for the deck. Just checking in, did you ever get that handled?"
No offer. No discount. No pressure. Just a human closing a loop.
A real number of them never did get it handled. Life happened, they meant to call you back, they got busy, and now they feel a little bad about it and you just made it easy.
These people already trusted you enough to invite you out and ask what it would cost. That is the warmest list you will ever own, it's free, and it's sitting in a folder.
Try it on twenty of them this week and come back and tell me what happened. This one surprises people every time.

headline: Your customers stopped answering the phone. They didn't stop wanting to buy.
fb: Your customers stopped answering unknown numbers. They did not stop wanting to hire you.

This trips up a lot of good operators. You call back, no answer, you leave a voicemail, nothing, and you conclude the lead was junk. It wasn't. They just don't answer numbers they don't recognize, and neither do you.
The fix is boring: call, and if there's no answer, immediately text. "Hey [name], Sarah from [business], just tried you about the water heater. Happy to text if that's easier."
Response rates on that second step are not close. People who ignored the call will reply to the text in four minutes.
And when they text back, keep texting. Don't insist on getting them on the phone because that's how you prefer to sell. Sell the way they want to buy.
Anyone else notice their phone answer rate falling off a cliff the last few years? What are you doing about it?

headline: Load your site on cell service, not office wifi. Count the seconds.
fb: Pull up your own website on your phone right now, on cell service, not your office wifi. Count the seconds until you can actually read something.
More than three and you're losing a real share of the people who tapped. They don't wait. They hit back and tap the next result, and if that tap came from an ad, you paid for it.
It's almost always the same cause. Enormous photos nobody compressed. Somebody uploaded the raw files straight off a camera and every visitor on a phone downloads all of it.
Free fix path: run your URL through Google PageSpeed Insights. Takes thirty seconds and hands you the actual list of what's wrong. Send that list to whoever built your site.
You do not need to understand any of it. You just need to make somebody fix it.
Post your load time below and I'll tell you if it's a problem. Test it on cell, not wifi, that's the whole point.

headline: I call your business on camera and tell you what I heard. Volunteers only.
fb: TEARDOWN TUESDAY. Volunteers only, and I mean that.

Drop your business below. I'll pick one, pull up your website and Google profile, and call your line on camera as a regular customer. Then I tell you the one thing I'd fix first, and I give you the free version of the fix even when there's a paid version.
Last week's takeaway, and I'm keeping a tally: I've called nineteen businesses for these now and five answered.
Five out of nineteen. In a market where everybody says it's slow and leads are expensive.
The bar is on the floor out here. That's not an insult, it's the single best news in this group, because stepping over it costs almost nothing.
Who's up? 👇
firstComment: Everything I post here goes deeper in the free group: {{GROUP_URL}}. No pitching, no spam, just the moves that put money back in a small business.

headline: One jobsite photo is a week of posts. It's already on your phone.
fb: You take before-and-after photos on every job and then do nothing with them. Four-minute fix.

Drop the photo into AI and say: "I'm a [trade] in [town]. Write a short Facebook post about this job aimed at local homeowners. No hashtags, no hype, write like a normal person talking about their work. Then give me three variations."
One job, a week of posts.

And here's why this beats any clever marketing idea you'll read this month: people don't hire you because you posted a tip. They hire you because they saw you doing the work over and over until you were the obvious person to call when the thing broke.
Consistency beats cleverness, and the photos are already sitting on your phone from work you already did.
One rule: read it before you post it and cut anything that sounds like a brochure. If it has the word "solutions" in it, start over.

headline: Your best week is also your leakiest week. That's the ceiling.
fb: Here's the trap nobody warns you about when you start a business.
When you're slow, you answer every call on the first ring. You're helpful, you follow up, you send the quote that night.
When you're slammed, which is exactly when the calls are worth the most, you're on a roof or under a sink or in somebody's chair, and the phone rings out.
So your best week is also your leakiest week. And it feels great, because you're busy, and you never see the four jobs that called and went elsewhere.
That's the ceiling. Your growth is capped by your own two hands, and no amount of hustle raises it, because hustle is what's already using the hands.
Every business that broke through did the same thing in some form. They stopped requiring themselves to be the one who answers.
Anyone else feel this one? What's your busiest day of the week and what actually happens to the phone on it?

headline: What I got wrong about AI for small business.
fb: Things I was wrong about, since this group is supposed to be useful and not a highlight reel.

I thought owners would want to build it themselves. Give them the prompts, the tools, the how. Most don't, and it's not laziness. It's that a person doing forty things already does not want a forty-first, even a good one. The people who DO want to build it are wonderful and they're maybe one in ten.

I thought the hard part was the technology. It's not, it hasn't been for a while. The hard part is that a small business's actual knowledge (your prices, your service area, your standards, the way you talk) lives entirely in the owner's head and has never been written down anywhere. That's the real work and no software skips it.

I thought people wanted to save time. Mostly they want to stop feeling behind. Those sound the same and they are not. Saving an hour on a task you hate feels like nothing if the pile is still there when you look up.
Still figuring it out. What have you been wrong about in your own business? The good version of that question, not the fake-humble one.

headline: Record yourself doing the job once. That's your training manual.
fb: You've been meaning to write down how things work here for about four years. Here's how to do it in an afternoon.
Next time you do the thing, talk through it out loud and record it on your phone. Opening the shop. Running the estimate. Closing out a job. Whatever the new person always gets wrong.
Then drop the recording into AI and say: "Turn this into a step-by-step checklist a brand new employee could follow, in plain language, and flag anything I skipped or assumed."
That last part is the gold. It catches the fifteen things you do automatically and never mention, which is exactly the stuff new hires get wrong and then get yelled at for.
One afternoon, five recordings, and you have the manual you've been meaning to write since 2022.
What's the one job at your company that only exists in one person's head? That's the first one to record.

headline: Call your three biggest competitors tonight. You're finding out one thing.
fb: Twenty-minute assignment for tonight, and it's the most useful thing you'll do this month.
Call your three biggest competitors after hours, as a customer. You're finding out exactly one thing: who answers.
Usually the answer is nobody, and you'll feel a lot better about yourself for about ten seconds.
Sometimes one of them answers. And that one is quietly eating everybody's evening and weekend work, and the rest of the market is standing around saying it's a slow year.

Then sit with the second half of this. Your customers run this exact test on you. Every single evening. They don't call you and wait, they call down the list until a human picks up, and then they stop calling.
Whoever answers wins. That's not strategy, that's arithmetic.
Do it tonight and report back. How many of your three picked up? I'll collect the results, I want the industry breakdown.

headline: Thirty days, zero dollars, four afternoons. The whole list.
fb: If you handed me your business for thirty days and said find money without spending any, this is the whole list, in order. Save it.
WEEK 1. Find out how many calls you actually miss. Just the number, from your phone bill or your carrier app. Most owners have never looked and it is the single most upsetting number in the business.
WEEK 2. Turn on missed-call text-back (it's already in the system you pay for) and fix your Google review link so it opens the review box in one tap instead of four. Both free, both an afternoon.
WEEK 3. Text every quote from the last twelve months that never closed. One line: "Did you ever get that handled?" No pitch, no discount.
WEEK 4. Paste all your bad reviews into AI at once and ask for the single most common pattern. Fix that one thing. It's almost never what you've been defending, it's almost always communication.
Zero dollars. Four afternoons. And it's more than most businesses will do all year, which is the entire reason it works.
Start with week one. Post your missed-call number when you find it. I promise you it's worse than you think, and I promise you're not the only one.
